title: #WelcomeAfghans: Key figures - Canada.ca
content:
## #WelcomeAfghans: Key figures
The Government of Canada has welcomed over 40,000 refugees and vulnerable Afghans to Canada. We’ve done this through several programs, including
- the Special Immigration Measures Program for Afghans who assisted the Government of Canada
- a humanitarian program
- a permanent residence pathway for extended family members of former interpreters
We’re continuing to process existing applications through these special programs on a priority basis to bring Afghans to Canada.
We’re no longer updating this page. The data on this page is approximate and was last updated on November 1, 2024. It can be used for research or reference purposes.
People who have arrived in Canada, under all streams, since August 2021

## Special Immigration Measures Program for Afghans who assisted the Government of Canada
Afghan nationals arriving through the Special Immigration Measures Program for those who assisted the Government of Canada fall under the government-assisted refugee program.

## Humanitarian program
Afghan nationals arriving through the humanitarian program fall under the government-assisted refugee and privately sponsored refugee programs. We’re working with trusted international and Canadian partners to implement this new humanitarian program. Afghan nationals don’t apply directly to us for this program.

## Pathway for extended family members of former interpreters
For extended family members to be eligible for this permanent residence pathway , the interpreters must
- already live in Canada and
- have resettled in Canada under public policies from 2009 or 2012

## Afghans who have arrived in Canada by destination province and community
The map and table below were last updated on October 27, 2023.
The data show the number of Afghans that have arrived in Canada through the following special programs:
- government-assisted refugees (GARs) resettling in communities where there is an IRCC-funded resettlement service provider organization The Province of Quebec receives separate funding to provide similar services as part of the Canada-Quebec Accord.
- The Province of Quebec receives separate funding to provide similar services as part of the Canada-Quebec Accord.
- privately sponsored refugees (PSRs) settling in the same community as their sponsor
- extended family members going to the community where their family member lives
Figures between 0 and 10 are shown as “--”. All other figures are rounded to the nearest 5. As a result of rounding, data may not sum to the totals indicated. This is done to prevent individuals from being identified when IRCC data is compiled and compared to other publicly available statistics.
Number of extended family members
Total number of people
Prince Edward Island
Newfoundland and Labrador
Newfoundland and Labrador
There are also 70 people whose destination communities could not be confirmed and are not included.
For privacy, and for the safety and security of those involved, we can’t provide details on specific cases.
